narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 41% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your second-decile rankings in both first-year and third-year school grades—well below the typical student's fifth- or sixth-decile standing—along with your LSAT score of 34.5, which is a bit lower than the average of around 36.7. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.6 stands out positively, exceeding the dataset average of 3.21, while your middle family income bracket aligns closely with typical applicants, and being a full-time White female student matches the most common demographic patterns in our data. When the model weighed all these factors, your third-year grade decile had the strongest influence, pushing the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure, followed closely by your first-year decile and then your LSAT score. Your family income, racial background, undergraduate GPA, gender, and full-time status each played smaller roles, all nudging slightly toward the same concerning outcome, though none were decisive on their own. In essence, the model saw your early and later school performance as the biggest red flags compared to peers who tend to rank higher in their classes. While your strong undergrad grades offered some counterbalance, they weren't enough to offset those lower deciles and LSAT in the model's view. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just how your specifics stacked up against patterns from thousands of past students. To boost your chances next time, focus on actionable steps like targeted test prep to retake and improve your LSAT, as scores can often rise with practice. Strengthening your academic deciles through better coursework or electives could also help if you're still in school. Building a stronger narrative around your undergrad success in essays or recommendations might highlight your resilience. Ultimately, many students in similar spots succeed by addressing these academic hurdles head-on—consider bar prep resources early or even community college bridges to law programs for a fresh start. Keep pushing; your above-average GPA shows you have the foundation to turn this around.
